bridges.titleA: Bifurcation mechanism at a sustain point of a long narrow economy
bridges.titleB: Scalarization of Charged Black Hole in Gauss-Bonnet Extended Starobinsky-Maxwell Gravity
bridges.mechanism: Both perform a bifurcation analysis of the equilibrium/solution branches of a nonlinear system as a control parameter is varied: paper A tracks a symmetry-breaking 'sustain point' where a uniform population state gives way to emergent twin-city branches, and paper B tracks critical horizon radii where scalarized black-hole solutions emerge, split, and reconnect into a multi-branch structure. The transferable machinery — parametrized solution branches with critical points marking branch emergence and splitting (spontaneous scalarization is itself a bifurcation off the bald solution) — maps role-for-role, so this is shared mechanism rather than mere shared 'branch/bifurcation' vocabulary.

bridges.titleA: Controllable Thouless Pumping Switching Dynamics of Gap Solitons Mediated by Finite Bogoliubov Excitations
bridges.titleB: Right-Handed Neutrino Production by an Axion-like Inflaton: Implications for Leptogenesis
bridges.mechanism: Both are governed by the same transferable machinery: a mode with a time-dependent effective spectrum driven non-adiabatically produces excitations, counted as Bogoliubov occupation in the instantaneous (Hamiltonian-diagonal) basis when the adiabatic theorem breaks down. In A the near-adiabatic ramp of the periodic-potential phase excites finite Bogoliubov quasiparticles that deplete the soliton; in B the rolling/oscillating inflaton drives non-adiabatic production of neutrino quanta whose occupation is defined in the Hamiltonian-diagonal basis, so the adiabatic-breakdown / Bogoliubov-transformation core is shared even though the topological (Chern-number) and leptogenesis superstructures differ.

bridges.titleA: Testing string theory with combined cosmological probes: a case study for dark matter gravitons
bridges.titleB: Parametric Diffraction-Based Object Sensing: Modeling, Estimation, and Fundamental Limits
bridges.mechanism: Both are the same estimation-theoretic machinery: infer the parameters of a physical forward model from noisy observations and quantify the fundamental achievable precision via Fisher information / the Cramer-Rao bound. Paper A's 'forecast' of the dark-sector parameters for a Stage-IV survey is precisely a Fisher/CRB forecast, the same object Paper B derives explicitly (ML estimator approaching the CRB) for blockage and DoA sensing, so the estimation framework maps role-for-role despite the disjoint cosmology-vs-wireless domains.

bridges.titleA: Higher-order Spillover Effects Under Partial Interference
bridges.titleB: Kernel weighted importance sampling for off-policy evaluation in contextual bandits
bridges.mechanism: Both estimate the expected outcome under a hypothetical/target assignment distribution from data drawn under a different actual distribution via importance-sampling / inverse-probability weighting: paper A's Horvitz-Thompson and Hajek estimators are exactly (unnormalized) importance sampling and its self-normalized 'weighted importance sampling' variant that paper B uses for off-policy evaluation, and both must correct for/absorb bias when the assumed generating mechanism (interference set / exposure mapping vs. behaviour policy) is misspecified.

bridges.titleA: Emergent Generalization by Representation Learning in Artificial Neural Networks
bridges.titleB: Choice at Finite Capacity: The Bounded Agent as an Information Channel and the Recovery of Walrasian Demand
bridges.mechanism: Both instantiate the same information-bottleneck / finite-capacity information-channel (rate-distortion) machinery: a system compresses a high-dimensional world into a capacity-limited representation, a single capacity parameter interpolates between a full-capacity limit (memorisation / the Walrasian consumer) and a degenerate low-capacity limit (pure habit), and each paper's central claim is that the emergent structured behaviour (generalisation in A; Slutsky-symmetric, downward-sloping demand in B) arises causally from the compression itself rather than from the conventional explanation.
